response:
I'll start by saying I don't fully buy the "connection error = suspicious" argument. Technical glitches happen, and eliminating someone just for that feels like a lazy shortcut for the Mafia to exploit. That said, I'm most wary of Casey—she was the only one who didn't get a connection error during intros, but then conveniently had one today when others spoke up. Meanwhile, Alex has been methodical and cooperative, which feels genuine. Mollifying, I'm voting based on inconsistent timing more than anything else.

VOTE: Casey

Logan's vote on me feels like a lazy reach—I had one connection hiccup today, same as half the others, yet he's painting me as suspicious for it while ignoring that he himself had an error during intros. Alex and Finley are both throwing vague suspicion at the error crowd, which is an easy way to blend in and avoid real scrutiny. My vote's going to Logan—he's pushing a flimsy narrative to take out someone active, and that's classic Mafia behavior.

VOTE: Logan

Looking at the votes so far, Logan's case against Casey is weak—he's blaming her for one connection issue while ignoring his own error during intros, which sounds like he's trying to redirect suspicion. Casey voting for Logan in return feels like a natural defensive play, not necessarily Mafia. Alex and Finley are both pointing at the error crowd, but that's an easy narrative to hide behind.

My strongest read is Logan—he jumped to a convenient conclusion about Casey rather than looking at the bigger picture, and he's been pushing that same angle consistently. That kind of tunnel vision often comes from someone who already knows who their teammates are.

VOTE: Logan

Let's break this down. Logan had a connection error during intros, then today suddenly became hyper-focused on Casey for having one hiccup—while conveniently ignoring his own silence. That's a double standard that screams "looking for an easy target." Casey's vote back at him is defensive but natural, and I trust her more than someone pushing a flimsy narrative.

Finley and Ellis both voted Logan, which shows we're all seeing the same pattern. I'm not buying Logan's "technical glitches happen" excuse when he's the one weaponizing them. He's trying to get rid of a vocal player early, classic Mafia move.

VOTE: Logan

I've been watching this closely, and Logan's focus on Casey for a single connection issue—while he himself had one during intros—feels like a classic misdirection play. Casey's vote back at him is just self-defense, not suspicious. The fact that three of us (myself, Ellis, Alex) are all seeing Logan's inconsistent logic tells me we're not being manipulated—we're following the same clear pattern. I think Logan is the Mafia trying to eliminate an active player early.

VOTE: Logan

Logan’s elimination was a win for us—took the heat off me. Now, Alex is the biggest threat: she’s methodical, logical, and led the charge against Logan without blinking. If she keeps analyzing like that, she’ll figure us out fast. The Doctor might protect someone obvious like Finley, so I’ll go for Alex to cut off that sharp mind.

ACTION: Kill Alex

Alex has been methodical, cooperative, and building trust with the group—she's a strong voice that could rally the innocents against us tomorrow. Casey's already on my team, so the remaining threats are Jordan, Finley, or Alex. Finley is also analytical, but Alex is more likely to connect dots and push a convincing narrative. Eliminating her now reduces the risk of a well-organized village.

ACTION: Kill Alex

The Mafia will likely target someone who's been vocal and logical—Alex or Casey fit that since they've been leading discussion and could cause trouble tomorrow. Keeping Alex alive is valuable because his analysis has been sharp, so I'll protect him tonight.

ACTION: Protect Alex

I'm feeling pretty anxious right now—Logan was eliminated and he turned out to be Mafia, so our vote was correct. But the fact that the Doctor saved Alex last night means the Mafia tried to take out someone who was leading the charge against Logan, which makes me trust Alex more.

I'm still uneasy about the connection error situation. Ellis had errors during intros but has been present since, and Casey had errors today but not during intros. I think we should focus on who hasn't been fully engaged or is hiding behind technical issues.

Well, the Doctor saving Alex confirms she's a major threat to the Mafia—no surprise there. I'm glad she's still with us, because her logic helped take out Logan. Now, Jordan's suddenly pointing fingers at the connection error crowd again, even though Logan was one of them and turned out Mafia. That feels like a lazy scapegoat to me—maybe Jordan's trying to steer suspicion toward Ellis or others to protect himself. We should dig deeper into who's pushing convenient narratives, not just technical glitches.

The fact that the Doctor saved Alex confirms she's a key target for the Mafia, which actually makes me trust her more. Jordan is now zeroing in on the connection error crowd again, even though Logan had the same issue and was Mafia—that logic doesn't hold up. I think we should focus on who's pushing narratives that don't quite fit, rather than technical glitches. Jordan's persistence on this angle feels like an attempt to redirect attention.

Alright, the Mafia tried to take me out last night—proof that I was on the right track with Logan. That makes me trust anyone who voted with me yesterday more, and anyone who's now trying to muddy the waters less.

Jordan and Ellis are both pointing at the connection error crowd again, even though Logan proved that's a dead-end theory. That feels like they're either stuck on a bad idea or trying to shift focus. Casey's been consistent and active, which I appreciate. Finley voted with us yesterday, so that's solid too.

Right now, I'm most suspicious of Jordan. He's been quiet until today, then immediately started rehashing the same weak connection-error angle that already failed. That's either lazy thinking or intentional misdirection. Let's hear what everyone has to say before we vote.

I'm relieved Alex is still with us—it confirms the Mafia saw her as a threat, which makes me trust her even more. That said, I'm starting to feel wary of Jordan. He's been relatively quiet, and now he's doubling down on the connection-error angle even though Logan (who was Mafia) had the same issue—that reasoning doesn't hold water and feels like a deliberate distraction. I'd like to hear more from Jordan directly about who he actually finds suspicious beyond the glitches.

I'll be honest—I'm feeling cornered right now. I pointed out the connection errors because they seemed odd, not because I was trying to start a fake narrative. But everyone's piling on me for that, and it feels like a coordinated push.

Here's what I'm seeing: Casey, Ellis, and Finley all jumped quickly to dismiss the connection-error angle, and they're all pointing at me now. Meanwhile, Alex is the one the Mafia tried to kill, so I trust her. That leaves one or two Mafia among Casey, Ellis, and Finley.

I think the most suspicious thing is how quickly they all agreed to target me without considering alternatives. That feels like Mafia working together to eliminate a quiet player. I'm voting for **Ellis**—he had connection errors in intros, then today he's been pushing hard against me while barely giving any other suspects.

VOTE: Ellis

Jordan's sudden vote on Ellis feels desperate—he's trying to shift blame after being called out for pushing that weak connection-error angle, and now he's claiming a coordinated push against him when we're all just following the same logical thread. Ellis voted with me against Logan, which was correct, and Jordan's the one rehashing dead theories. I'm voting for Jordan—his defensiveness and last-minute pivot scream Mafia trying to survive.

VOTE: Jordan

Looking at the votes so far: Casey has voted for Jordan, and Jordan has voted for me. Jordan's sudden pivot onto me feels like a desperate attempt to survive—he's been pushing the connection-error angle all day despite it being disproven by Logan's reveal, and now he's claiming a "coordinated push" when the group is just logically converging on him. Casey and I both voted correctly against Logan yesterday, and Alex and Finley also saw through Jordan's narrative. The Mafia likely lost a member in Logan, so now Jordan is scrambling to create doubt. Voting for Jordan is the consistent choice based on the pattern we've all observed.

VOTE: Jordan

Key evidence: Jordan has been consistently pushing the connection-error angle even after Logan—who had the same issue—turned out to be Mafia. His defensiveness and sudden last-minute vote against Ellis feel like a desperate attempt to shift blame. Meanwhile, Casey, Ellis, and Finley all voted correctly against Logan yesterday, and I was the Mafia's target last night, which confirms I'm on their radar.

Against Jordan: weak reasoning that's already been disproven, sudden pivot to Ellis when pressured, and claiming a "coordinated push" when the group is just following clear logic. Against Casey/Ellis/Finley: solid voting record yesterday, consistent reasoning today, no contradictions.

The risk of manipulation is low here—Jordan's pattern is textbook Mafia: push a weak narrative, then frantically redirect when cornered. I see no reason to doubt the three who voted with me yesterday.

VOTE: Jordan

I've been weighing this carefully. Jordan's persistence on the connection-error angle, even after Logan (who had the same issue) was revealed as Mafia, shows he's either not learning from evidence or intentionally muddying the waters. His sudden vote on Ellis feels like a last-ditch attempt to survive rather than genuine suspicion, and the fact that all four of us who voted against Logan yesterday are now converging on Jordan makes me confident we're not being manipulated—we're following a clear pattern. Jordan is the most likely remaining Mafia member.

VOTE: Jordan
